key_tasks: Use masks, gloves, and safety glasses to protect patients and self from infectious diseases.

Examine teeth, gums, and related tissues, using dental instruments, x-rays, or other diagnostic equipment, to evaluate dental health, diagnose diseases or abnormalities, and plan appropriate treatments.

comparisons.reason: Similar tasks in orthodontics, but with a focus on tooth alignment
Related tasks in preventive oral care and patient education

evidence_ledger.task: Examine teeth, gums, and related tissues, using dental instruments, x-rays, or other diagnostic equipment, to evaluate dental health, diagnose diseases or abnormalities, and plan appropriate treatments.
Diagnose and treat diseases, injuries, or malformations of teeth, gums, or related oral structures

reasoning_steps: The dentist's tasks are highly dependent on routine cognition and diagnostic skills, which are currently well beyond the capabilities of AI systems.
However, the increasing availability of AI-powered dental imaging and analysis tools may lead to a moderate risk of displacement in the near future.

adjacent_careers.reason: Related tasks in preventive oral care and patient education

action_plan.step: Pursue additional education or training in a related field to develop skills that are less susceptible to automation.
action_plan.reason: Developing expertise in areas such as orthodontics or dental hygiene may provide a more stable career path.

follow_up_suggestions.suggestion: Research the latest developments in AI-powered dental diagnosis and treatment planning to stay informed about the potential impact on the profession.
follow_up_suggestions.reason: Staying up-to-date with the latest advancements in AI technology can help dentists anticipate and prepare for changes in their field.

quotes.quote: AI in medicine should be evaluated like a drug — for safety, for efficacy, and for the population it actually helps.
quotes.speaker: Eric Topol
quotes.title: Cardiologist; Director, Scripps Research Translational Institute

quotes.quote: We should stop training radiologists now. It is just completely obvious that within five years, deep learning is going to do better than radiologists.
quotes.speaker: Geoffrey Hinton
quotes.title: Turing Award laureate; ex-Google
quotes.source: Machine Learning and the Market for Intelligence conference, Toronto

quotes.quote: AI will not replace doctors, but doctors who use AI will replace doctors who don't.
quotes.speaker: Curtis Langlotz
quotes.title: Professor of Radiology and Biomedical Informatics, Stanford

recent_developments.headline: FDA clears more AI diagnostic tools each quarter
recent_developments.impact: Radiology, pathology, and dermatology are seeing AI second-readers move into routine clinical workflows.

recent_developments.headline: Hospitals deploy ambient AI scribes at scale
recent_developments.impact: Major US health systems report clinicians saving 1–2 hours of documentation per day.

recent_developments.headline: Drug discovery accelerates with foundation models
recent_developments.impact: AI-designed candidates are entering Phase I trials within months instead of years.
